speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Any time. This is tape five.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is December the 7th, 1995. This is an interview of Hedy Epstein by Julie Heifetz, in St. Louis, in the United States. Hedy, you were talking about the Nuremberg Trials, at which you worked. Were there any times at which you had any other contact with Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There are two situations that I can think of. This was-- I was already working on the trial of the doctors who performed medical experiments, but the international trial was still going on. And I sat in on one of the court sessions. And recess was called, but the defendants who were sitting in the dock were not allowed to leave. And I took it upon myself to stand in front of Goring. And he-- and I just-- I didn't say anything. I just stood there and looked at him-- stared at him. And he was beginning to feel increasingly uncomfortable. I could tell from his body language. He was squirming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he asked, finally, his German defense counsel, you know, who is this little one? What does she want? He didn't realize that I understood German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the defense counsel said to him, I don't know what she wants. I know she's from the prosecution. Don't say anything. Don't do anything. And I-- and I, of course-- I understood that also. And I was thinking to myself, here is little Hedy Wachenheimer from Kippenheim, standing in front of Goring and making him squirm. Not terribly long ago, if I would have had to stand in front of him, I would have been the one squirming. It was maybe a foolish kind of a power trip that I was on, but it was something that I felt I wanted to do at that time. The other thing is kind of-- has a lighter side to it. Some of the prisoners, or some-- and some of the witnesses that were on loan to us from the British or other governments, who were also conducting trials, were in the jail, which was part of the Palace of Justice. And the German defense counsels had access, of course, to them, and would interview them or meet with them. And periodically, someone from the prosecution would accompany them just to make sure that nothing that shouldn't happen takes place. And on one occasion, I was asked to accompany this German defense counsel. And he was interviewing a witness who was on loan to us from the British government. And usually, the German defense counsel would have introduced me by name and that I was from the prosecution, but he somehow forgot to do this. And so the prisoner probably thought that I was his secretary, because I had a pad with me and a pen. And then at some point, the defense counsel said, oh, I forgot to bring something from my office, which was in the Palace of Justice. I'm going to run back and get it. I'll be right back. And just in the interim, while he was gone and the prisoner was behind bars, he said to me in German that, you know, 'I'm going to ask you for a favor, and you are going to be richly rewarded if you do this. And it's very important that you do it and that it's done right. 'You know that the mail here is censored. And I have a letter here which has to get out, but it cannot go through the censorship. So if you will take this and put it in your pad that you have here, and then when you go home tonight, please mail it for me. And I will know that it's been delivered as it should be, and you will be very richly rewarded.' And I said, sure. I'd be very glad to do this. And he handed me the letter, and I put it in my pad. And we chit chatted some more. And then the German defense counsel comes back and says, oh, I forgot. This is Hedy Wachenheimer from the prosecution. [LAUGHTER] And the witness just-- oh, my! I'm so sorry. And he was profusely apologetic and asked me to return the letter to him, and that she shouldn't have done this, and, you know, so on and so forth. And I said to him, that's perfectly all right. I will make sure that the letter gets to its proper destination. And I did not return it to him. He begged and he pleaded, but I wouldn't. And I turned it over to the censorship division. And apparently, it contained some information that was helpful to us in then locating someone we had not been able to locate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hedy, after the trials were over, did you ever go back to Kippenheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was back in Kippenheim the first time in 19-- in August 1947. I had wanted to go back before. But in fact, one time I was actually on the train and then got off the first time the train stopped, because I just could not face going back. And what-- what it was that I couldn't face was that I, on one level, I was hoping that my parents would be in Kippenheim. But I also knew on another level that they're not there. But if I go back and they're not there, I can no longer tell myself my parents are in Kippenheim. And so that's why it took me until August '47 to go back. And it was a very frightening experience for me. I felt like I was back during the Nazi period. And as I walked past our house, I sort of only looked at it out of the corner of my eye, because I was afraid somebody might recognize me. I went because I-- I had hoped initially to-- to go to the house and go inside, but I was afraid to. And so I went to the village hall and talked to the mayor. And I didn't know who he was. He could have been a former Nazi for all I knew. And I don't know whether he was or not. And I explained who I am and why I was there. And he took me over to our house. But by the time we got there, I did not want to go in. I wanted to remember it the way it was. Even if my memory was not proper or was false memory, I wanted to remember it that way. And I was back in the village again in 1970 with my husband and my son, but again, I did not want to go in the house. And then, in 1990, when the village invited all the survivors to come back, I was there with my son, and my son encouraged me to go in. And I was again reluctant to go in. And he said, oh, Mom. I have a better camera than you, and I can take some photographs. And I really would like to see what it looks like. And so I rang the doorbell, and a woman opened the door and let us in. And the amazing thing was when we went into the bedroom, which was also my parents' bedroom, the light fixture was still the same light fixture that my parents had. They had renovated the house the both inside and outside, so it looked different than the way I remembered it. But she had photographs of the before and after, which she shared with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after you had been in Germany for your-- for the trials, and you went back after that, what happened-- if you can just tell us your transition here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. In March 194-- in March 1948, my job ended, and I went back to England. And I stayed in England about 10 weeks, and then came to the United States in late May 1948. And I lived in New York first-- lived with my Uncle Manfred and Aunt Else for about two weeks. That was my mother's brother and his wife. And they showered me-- literally showered me-- with love, and care, and affection, to the point where I really could not deal with it. It was too much. They were both working and did not want me to work. Every day, they came-- each of them came home with a present for me. My aunt got upset if I made my own bed. I guess they wanted to make up for what I had missed. And I told them. I said, you know-- I begged them not to do this. But they could not stop, and I could not accept it. And so I said, I want to have an ongoing good relationship with you. And in order for this to happen, I need to move out. I need to be on my own. I've been on my own essentially since I've been 14 and 1/2, certainly since I've been 16. And they were very unhappy about that. But they eventually-- I moved out, and they understood eventually that because we had a continuing and ongoing relationship until they both died, that I needed to do-- to do this. I needed to be my own person.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since then, it's-- this is a very quick trip through the rest of your life. But can you tell us what life has been like since then?

speaker: interviewee
text: The jobs that I had for many years, and I only realized it in retrospect, were really jobs that enabled me to still be looking for my parents. The first job was for the New York Association for New Americans, which brought over displaced persons. And I would look at each person that arrived. Is this my mother? Is this my father, my uncle, my aunt?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you? And did-- you know, and if they would have been in the same place as my parents, I would have asked, you know, did you know my parents? So I was still, through my work, continuing my search for my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1952, I was married to Felix Wolf-- W-O-L-F. And it was a very short marriage that ended unhappily. And then, I was remarried in November 1955 to Arnold Epstein, who died in December, on December 21st, 1977. We had two sons. One of them now lives in another state, and he's married, and has-- we have-- he has two children, two daughters. In fact, I would like to dedicate this interview to my oldest granddaughter, because it's her 10th birthday today. I had another son who was born in 1961 but lived only a very short time, and is buried in Ohio, where we lived at the time. The marriage to Arnold Epstein also ended in divorce in 1974. But when he became ill, he came back, because he wanted to quote, unquote, 'come home and die at home.' And he died in my home. He had cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all this time, you've continued working for various organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I've worked-- my work has been with human rights or civil rights organizations. And I retired on March 31st of this year, of 1995. And I envisioned that a retirement would-- one of the pleasures of retirement would be to be able to read a lot. That hasn't really happened. In fact, I'm busier now than I've been when I was working. One of the things that I am doing, and I've done it for the last 25 or 26 years-- I have given talks about the subject that we covered during this interview. I've also talked about the medical experiments in much greater detail than I covered today. And I've now been doing this on a much greater scale. However, I noticed in April and May of this year, I was doing an awful lot of talking, sometimes three and four times a week, and I began to have Holocaust-related nightmares. And I realized it was just-- I was doing it too much, too often, in a short span of time. And so I now learned to space myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hedy, I know that we could talk on for hours and hours. I wish we could, but could you tell us at the end of this interview, before we do the photographs, is there anything else that you would like to say that we have not yet touched upon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I would like to talk about a dream, a recurring dream that I had. And for a long, long time, I didn't know what the dream was about. I just knew it's that dream again, and I would wake up extremely agitated and in tears sometimes. And then, in 19-- the middle of the 1960s, I dreamt again the same dream. And perhaps for the first time I was-- I verbalized while I was dreaming. And I was saying, I didn't do it. I'm not guilty. And my husband woke me up and said, you're just having a nightmare. You're dreaming. You're all right. Nothing is happening. And perhaps because he interrupted me in the middle of the dream, I knew for the first time what I was dreaming. And I dreamt that I had put my parents on the train, just the way they had put me on the train. And when they put me on the train, they were on the platform. But in my dream, I was on the platform. And I could see the train leaving. And they were looking out the window, and they were getting smaller and smaller. And finally, they were gone. And I also knew that the train was going to a concentration camp and going to their extermination. And I just spilled that really fast. And I immediately understood what the dream was about, that it was my guilt for surviving. And I did not dream that dream again until a few days before my then, I guess, ex-husband died, when I was aware I'm dreaming it. And I was able to wake myself up from the dream and was saying, and you're not responsible. You're not guilty because this man is dying. And I've not dreamt it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Hedy, thank you for all that you've told us. You've made a great contribution to this world since, and to this project, by giving the interview. If we could then stop, and we'll show your pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much. And I appreciate and feel honored being part of this project and being interviewed by you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wonderful job. He gets the tape up to speed. You can just describe that and, you know, if you can do it in a minute or so.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1980-- September 1980, I visited Camp de Gurs, the camp where my parents had first been sent to. And I spent the entire day there. And at the end of the day, I left. And I was looking down on the ground, being very contemplative about what I had felt and experienced that day. And there were a whole bunch of rocks on the side of the road. And all of a sudden, I felt that this one rock said to me, 'Pick me up. Take me home.' And I picked it up. And I looked at it, dusted it off. And I decided it was a nice rock, and it would be a souvenir, if-- if you want to call it that, from this place. I also knew at that time that the following year, there was going to be the first international gathering of Holocaust survivors in Israel. And I was going to attend. And they had asked that people bring stones from the locale where they live and engrave them, if they wish, with the names of their loved ones who didn't survive. And so that was probably perhaps in the back of my mind. And I thought, well, this is one of the stones that I think I will take. And I collected stones subsequently from the other concentration camps, and decided those would be the stones I take to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I was back in the United States, I was showing the different stones to people I was talking to. And this one person, all of a sudden, started to cry. And I said-- and I said, why are you crying? I don't need to talk about this anymore. I don't want to upset you. And she said, 'Don't you know what's on the stone?' And I said, 'What is on the stone?'

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, 'There's a Star of David on the stone.'

speaker: interviewee
text: And for the first time, I saw it myself. And I then took it to a geologist, because I wanted to know, is it man-made, or is it nature-made? And he tested it, and it's nature-made. But it's just an-- an incredible thing to have found that-- That's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at this particular place in Camp de Gurs. The other stones I took to Israel. I could not part with this stone. It's very special to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, that's great. OK. One, two, three. What do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Hugo Wachenheimer, H-U-G-O. Wachenheimer is W-A-C-H-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R. This photograph was taken in the summer of 1938, at which time my father was almost 49 years old. This is my mother, Ella Wachenheimer, E-L-L-A, Wachenheimer, W-A-C-H-E-N-H-E-I-M-E-R. Her maiden name was Eichel, E-I-C-H-E-L. This photograph was taken in the summer of 1938, at which time my mother was 44.

speaker: interviewer
text: A little glare on there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. OK. My parents were-- OK. And go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: The pictures of my parents were taken in Germany. This is a birthday card that I received in England-- my first birthday-- my 15th birthday. My father made this card. The ring with the candles on it is-- represents a wooden ring that I had at home that was always used on my birthday and was put on top of the cake with the appropriate number of candles in it. My father's English wasn't perfect. But my father was a very talented person. And he did all kinds of things, and this was one of them. And it was taken-- this was made in Germany and sent to me in England.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] OK. Stand by-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an identification tag that my father made up for me to wear-- on a string around my neck when I went on the children's transport to England, on May 18th, 1939. It has my maiden name on it, and the village where I was born, and-- I mean, where I lived, and my-- the permit number, number 5580. England took in almost 10,000 children between December 2, 1938, and the end of August, 1939. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, Terry. Rolling. Stand by. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of me taken in August 1938, when I was 14 years old. It was taken in Germany and served as my passport picture when I went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tape rolling. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken in August 1937 because of my grandmother's 75th birthday, my-- the-- my father's mother. And it was taken in my grandmother's backyard. The grape arbor in the back is something that my father-- or my father trained the grapes to grow in this particular way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's try this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Hugo Wachenheimer; my mother, Ella Wachenheimer; my uncle, Adolf, A-D-O-L-F, Strauss, S-T-R-A-U-S-S, who is the husband of my father's younger sister, Sophie, S-O-P-H-I-E, Strauss nee Wachenheimer. This is my father's oldest sister, Hilda, H-I-L-D-A, Weil, W-E-I-L, nee Wachenheimer. This is her youngest daughter, Tony Weil, T-O-N-Y, W-E-I-L-L. And this is Hilda's husband, Tony's father, Theo, T-H-E-O, Weil. This is my aunt, Kaethe, K-A-E-T-H-E, the wife of my father's youngest brother. This is my grandmother, my father's mother, Lina, L-I-N-A, Wachenheimer, and this was taken on her 75th birthday. This is my cousin, Lisa Weil, L-I-S-A, W-E-I-L-L. She is one half of a set of twins. We will see the other twin in a minute. This is my uncle, Oskar, the husband of Kaethe Wachenheimer and my father's youngest brother. O-S-K-A-R is his name. And this is me, Hedy Wachenheimer, now Epstein. This is my grandfather, Heinrich, H-E-I-N-R-I-C-H, Eichel, E-I-C-H-E-L, my mother's father. This is Erna Weil, E-R-N-A, the other half of the twin-- of the set of twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph that was taken in Camp de Gurs-- C-A-M-P, new word, D-E, new word, capital G-U-R-S, of family members. Once a month, they were allowed initially to spend one hour together. Other than that, men and women were separated by barbed wire. I don't know who took the picture or how it got out. My--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is one of the ones you spoke of earlier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] My parents are in the upper right-hand corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- OK. OK. And go right ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first letter I received from my father and other family members after they were deported to Camp de Gurs, on August 20-- I'm sorry-- on October 22, 1940. The letter is dated November 20, 1940. And my father, amongst other things, says, 'In the meantime, we have changed our address.' I guess he was either afraid to say that they had been deported, or it was because he wanted to shield me from knowing that. This is the new address, which he refers to in the letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you want to just do a little tilt down? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Let's look at the-- some of the handwriting. That's good. OK. Is it all the way down already? Keep rolling. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is-- my mother's last letter, written on September 1, 1942, in which she informs me that she's going to be deported the following day, and tells me that my father was deported on August 12, 1942, and that she doesn't know where he was sent to. But she's hoping to meet him somewhere. And then, I will-- and she goes-- she states, 'And then I will carry our lot-- we will carry our lot, no matter how difficult it may be, with dignity and with courage.' And she's admonishing me to be good and honest at all times, and asking me to never forget my parents, to never forget any of them. And then she says, you know, even if it takes a long time before we hear from each other again, she's hoping that we will be reunited somewhere, sometime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very good. OK. Stand by, and-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is one more communication from my mother, and it is the very last one, dated September 4, 1942. And she's saying that she's traveling to the East, and she's saying from Montauban. That's M-O-N-T-A-U-B-A-N, which is the postmark on the reverse side of this post card. She's sending me final goodbyes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I suspect that this postcard was tossed out of the train, or any other mode of transportation that she was on, and was sent to me by a very kind soul whom I can never say thank you to.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Fine? And what do we have here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a letter dated 19th of June, 1956, and it's from a French organization informing me that on the 11th of September, 1942, my mother was deported to the concentration camp, Auschwitz. At the same time, I received an identical letter pertaining to my father, telling me that he too was deported to Auschwitz, on September 1942. However, I subsequently learned he was already sent to Auschwitz on August 19, 1942. And neither one of my parents, nor any of my other family members, all of whom were sent to Auschwitz between August and September 1942, have ever been heard from. There were some, like my mother's father, who died already in December, 1940, in Camp de Gurs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] OK? OK.
